title: Cinnamomum tenuipile
what_description: Cinnamomum tenuipile is a species of evergreen tree in the family Lauraceae, native to Southeast Asia. It has a straight or slightly curved trunk, with a grey or brown bark, and a branch system with a spreading crown, typically reaching heights of 20-30 meters. The leaves are elliptical to ovate in shape, with a leathery texture, measuring 10-20 cm in length and 4-8 cm in width, with a bright green color on the upper surface and a pale green color on the lower surface, and are arranged oppositely on the branches. The inflorescences are axillary, with clusters of small flowers, while the fruits are drupes, with a single seed, measuring 1-2 cm in diameter, and a red or purple color when ripe. The wood of Cinnamomum tenuipile is valued for its timber, used for furniture and construction.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Cinnamomum tenuipile are inflammation, digestive issues, and hypertension, which are alleviated by its anti-inflammatory properties that reduce swelling and pain. Additionally, diabetes management is improved due to its ability to regulate blood sugar levels. Arthritis symptoms are alleviated by its anti-inflammatory and analgesic properties, while fungal infections are prevented by its antifungal properties. Cinnamomum tenuipile also exhibits antibacterial properties that combat bacterial infections and gastritis, and its antioxidant properties help to reduce the risk of cancer. Furthermore, its analgesic properties provide pain relief, and its antimicrobial properties prevent high cholesterol, cardiovascular disease, gastroenteritis, gastrointestinal issues, cough suppression, and bacterial infections.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Cinnamomum tenuipile are attributed to its rich content of bioactive compounds, which include flavonoids, saponins, and volatile oils. The antioxidant property of Cinnamomum tenuipile is evident due to the presence of flavonoids, which scavenge free radicals and mitigate oxidative stress, thereby protecting against cell damage and disease. Additionally, the antimicrobial property of this plant is attributed to its volatile oils, which exhibit inhibitory effects against a range of microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi. The anti-inflammatory property of Cinnamomum tenuipile is a result of the saponins, which inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, thus reducing inflammation and alleviating pain. Furthermore, the antifungal property of this plant is associated with its flavonoids, which exhibit antifungal activities against certain pathogenic fungi, while its analgesic property is attributed to the volatile oils, which exhibit pain-relieving effects.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Cinnamomum tenuipile are cinnamaldehyde, linalool, β-caryophyllene, α-terpineol, and cinnamyl acetate. Cinnamaldehyde has been shown to exhibit antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities, as well as the ability to modulate the immune system. Linalool has been demonstrated to possess anxiolytic and sedative properties, as well as the ability to inhibit the growth of certain bacteria and fungi. β-Caryophyllene has been found to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, while also exhibiting potential as an anticancer agent. α-Terpineol has been shown to possess antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory activities, and cinnamyl acetate has been found to exhibit antioxidant properties, making these compounds important contributors to the healing effects of Cinnamomum tenuipile.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Cinnamomum tenuipile are cinnamon tea, cinnamon oil, cinnamon tincture, cinnamon powder, and cinnamon infusion. These preparations are made from the bark and leaves of the Cinnamomum tenuipile tree, which are rich in cinnamaldehyde, a compound with antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Cinnamon tea is made by steeping the bark and leaves in hot water, while cinnamon oil is obtained through steam distillation of the bark and leaves. Cinnamon tincture is a concentrated liquid extract of the bark and leaves, often used in traditional medicine, and cinnamon powder is obtained by drying and grinding the bark and leaves. Cinnamon infusion, also known as cinnamon decoction, is made by simmering the bark and leaves in water to release their bioactive compounds, which have been traditionally used to treat various health conditions.
side_effects_description: The possible side effect of using Cinnamomum tenuipile are inducing nausea due to its high concentration of coumarin, which can cause gastrointestinal irritation, developing skin irritation due to its oil's phototoxic properties, causing diarrhea due to its stimulant effect on the gut, experiencing allergic reactions such as hives and itching in rare cases, and increasing heart rate due to its potential interaction with cardiovascular medications, particularly beta blockers and blood thinners. Cinnamomum tenuipile's high coumarin content can lead to liver damage and kidney issues when consumed in large amounts or for extended periods. This plant's essential oil can also cause irritation to the mucous membranes, respiratory tract, and eyes. Ingesting large quantities of Cinnamomum tenuipile can lead to a range of adverse effects, including but not limited to, seizures, and respiratory failure. Pregnant women are advised to avoid using Cinnamomum tenuipile due to its potential to cause uterine contractions and miscarriage.
uses: Cinnamomum tenuipile, a lesser-known species of cinnamon, has been traditionally used in various cultures for its medicinal properties. Its bark, leaves, and twigs contain bioactive compounds that exhibit anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities. In traditional medicine, it is used to treat various ailments, including fever, digestive issues, and respiratory problems. The plant's essential oils have also been studied for their potential in wound healing and anti-cancer properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the therapeutic potential of Cinnamomum tenuipile and its safety for human use.
taxonomy: Cinnamomum tenuipile is a species of plant in the family Lauraceae. It is a type of evergreen tree native to Borneo. The classification of Cinnamomum tenuipile is further divided into the kingdom Plantae and the order Laurales. This plant is closely related to other species within the genus Cinnamomum, which includes various types of cinnamon and camphor trees. Cinnamomum tenuipile is a relatively rare and lesser-known species within its genus.
benefits: Cinnamomum tenuipile, a lesser-known species of cinnamon, is said to have various health benefits. The plant's essential oils have been found to have antimicrobial properties, which can help prevent infections and promote wound healing. Additionally, the plant's extracts have been shown to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of arthritis and other inflammatory conditions. Some studies have also suggested that Cinnamomum tenuipile may have antioxidant and anti-cancer properties, although further research is needed to confirm these findings. Overall, the health benefits of Cinnamomum tenuipile make it a potential natural remedy for various health conditions.
parts: Cinnamomum tenuipile is a species of evergreen tree in the cinnamon family, and like other members of the genus, its bark is a key component with medicinal properties. The bark of Cinnamomum tenuipile contains oils that have been traditionally used for their antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. The leaves of the tree are also used in some traditional medicine practices, although their medicinal properties are less well-studied than those of the bark. Additionally, the essential oils extracted from the bark and leaves of Cinnamomum tenuipile have shown potential in the treatment of various health conditions. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal properties and potential uses of Cinnamomum tenuipile.
